1:
"Wherefore laying aside all malice, and all guile, and hypocrisies, and envies, and all evil speakings," The witnesses note the call to lay aside the sins contrary to brotherly love. Gill notes since the persons addressed were born again and ought to love one another, he exhorts them to the disuse of vices disagreeable to their regenerate character and contrary to brotherly love. The witnesses note Peter charging that they lay aside all malice, guile, hypocrisies, envies, and evil speakings; the putting off of the sins that war against love — that Peter, following the call to fervent love (1:22), bids them strip off ("laying aside," as one casts off a filthy garment) the vices that destroy love and disgrace the new birth: "all malice" (ill-will and spite), "and all guile" (deceit and craft), "and hypocrisies" (false pretence), "and envies" (grudging others' good), "and all evil speakings" (slanders and backbitings); the witnesses note that these are all sins of the tongue and heart that break the bond of brotherly love, and the regenerate man, born of the incorruptible seed, must put them all away as inconsistent with his new nature; so that holiness begins with the laying aside of all that opposes love; laying aside all malice, and all guile, and hypocrisies, and envies, and all evil speakings. The witnesses note laying aside all malice, and all guile, and hypocrisies. The witnesses note and envies, and all evil speakings.

The witnesses note the putting off of the sins that war against love — malice, guile, hypocrisies, envies, evil speakings; the regenerate man casting them off as inconsistent with his new nature.

So the witnesses make the verse the laying aside of the sins contrary to love. The lesson the expositors draw is that the regenerate must strip off malice, guile, hypocrisy, envy, and slander, which destroy brotherly love. The witnesses note the laying aside: "laying aside all malice" — Gill notes casting off vices contrary to love; the laying aside; the casting off; the vices; the contrary to love; the strip off. The expositors observe the all malice: "all malice" — ill-will and spite; the all malice; the ill-will; the spite; the the heart; the the sin. The witnesses press the guile and hypocrisies: "and all guile, and hypocrisies" — deceit and false pretence; the guile; the hypocrisies; the deceit; the craft; the pretence. The expositors draw the lesson on the envies: "and envies" — grudging others' good; the envies; the grudging; the others' good; the envy; the the heart. The witnesses observe the evil speakings: "and all evil speakings" — slanders and backbitings; the evil speakings; the slanders; the backbitings; the the tongue; the the sin. The expositors note the break love: these sins break the bond of brotherly love; the break love; the the bond; the brotherly love; the destroy; the oppose. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the laying aside all malice and all guile and hypocrisies, the and envies and all evil speakings, and the putting off of sin. The verse thus records the laying aside of the sins contrary to love — teaching that the regenerate must strip off malice, guile, hypocrisy, envy, and slander.
2:
"As newborn babes, desire the sincere milk of the word, that ye may grow thereby:" The witnesses note the call to desire the word as babes desire milk. Gill notes the phrase describes the persons addressed, regenerate ones, lately born again, and carries in it an exhortation to desire the word with the eagerness with which a newborn babe desires its mother's milk. The witnesses note Peter charging that, as newborn babes, they desire the sincere milk of the word, that they may grow by it; the eager appetite for the pure word as the means of spiritual growth — that Peter, having bidden them lay aside corrupt things, now gives the positive nourishment: "as newborn babes" (those lately born again) "desire the sincere milk of the word" (long for, with the keen natural craving of an infant for milk, the pure, unadulterated word of God) "that ye may grow thereby"; the witnesses note the aptness of the figure — the word is the milk that nourishes the new-born soul, "sincere" (pure, unmixed with error or human corruption), and the babe must crave it earnestly, for by it, and by no other food, the soul grows up in grace; so that the believer's spiritual growth depends on a hearty appetite for the pure word of God; desire the sincere milk of the word, that ye may grow thereby. The witnesses note as newborn babes, desire the sincere milk of the word. The witnesses note that ye may grow thereby.

The witnesses note the call to desire the pure word as babes crave milk — the means of spiritual growth; the word the milk that nourishes the new-born soul, to be craved earnestly.

So the witnesses make the verse the appetite for the word as the means of growth. The lesson the expositors draw is that the new-born soul is to crave the pure word, by which alone it grows in grace. The witnesses note the newborn babes: "as newborn babes" — Gill notes those lately born again; the newborn babes; the lately born; the regenerate; the the new birth; the infants. The expositors observe the desire: "desire the sincere milk of the word" — long for it with an infant's craving; the desire; the long for; the the craving; the keen; the eager. The witnesses press the sincere milk: "the sincere milk of the word" — the pure, unadulterated word; the sincere milk; the pure; the unmixed; the the word; the unadulterated. The expositors draw the lesson on the grow thereby: "that ye may grow thereby" — that the soul may grow by it; the grow thereby; the the soul; the grow; the by it; the in grace. The witnesses observe the the milk nourishes: the word is the milk that nourishes the new-born soul; the the milk nourishes; the the word; the the soul; the new-born; the feeds. The expositors note the depends on appetite: spiritual growth depends on a hearty appetite for the word; the depends on appetite; the spiritual growth; the appetite; the the word; the hearty. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as newborn babes desire the sincere milk of the word, the that ye may grow thereby, and the appetite for the word. The verse thus records the appetite for the word as the means of growth — teaching that the new-born soul is to crave the pure word, by which alone it grows in grace.
3:
"If so be ye have tasted that the Lord is gracious." The witnesses note that the appetite for the word follows the taste of the Lord's grace. Gill notes reference is had to "O taste and see that the Lord is good"; by the Lord is meant the Lord Jesus Christ, who is gracious and amiable, lovely in his person, having a fulness of grace. The witnesses note Peter adding, "if so be ye have tasted that the Lord is gracious"; the experimental taste of Christ's grace that whets the appetite for his word — that Peter grounds the craving for the word (v.2) in a sweet experience already had: "if so be ye have tasted that the Lord is gracious" (as those who have once tasted, by faith, the graciousness, sweetness, and kindness of the Lord Jesus); the witnesses note the force of the figure — having tasted how gracious the Lord is, the soul longs for more of him in his word, even as one who has tasted a sweet food desires it again; so that the appetite for the word springs from a real, personal experience of Christ's grace, and the more the believer has tasted of his sweetness, the more he craves the milk of the word in which Christ is found; if so be ye have tasted that the Lord is gracious. The witnesses note if so be ye have tasted. The witnesses note that the Lord is gracious.

The witnesses note the experimental taste of Christ's grace that whets the appetite for his word — having tasted how gracious the Lord is, the soul longs for more of him in his word.

So the witnesses make the verse the taste of Christ's grace whetting the appetite. The lesson the expositors draw is that the craving for the word springs from a real experience of how gracious the Lord is. The witnesses note the tasted: "if so be ye have tasted" — Gill notes a taste by faith already had; the tasted; the by faith; the experience; the already; the had. The expositors observe the the Lord is gracious: "that the Lord is gracious" — the Lord Jesus, gracious and sweet; the the Lord is gracious; the Christ; the gracious; the sweet; the kind. The witnesses press the the figure: having tasted his sweetness, the soul longs for more; the the figure; the tasted; the longs for more; the sweet food; the desires. The expositors draw the lesson on the more of him: the soul craves more of Christ in his word; the more of him; the craves; the in his word; the longs; the Christ. The witnesses observe the personal experience: the appetite springs from a real, personal experience of grace; the personal experience; the real; the of grace; the the appetite; the springs. The expositors note the the more tasted: the more tasted, the more craved; the the more tasted; the the more craved; the his sweetness; the the word; the desires. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if so be ye have tasted, the that the Lord is gracious, and the taste of grace. The verse thus records the taste of Christ's grace whetting the appetite — teaching that the craving for the word springs from a real experience of how gracious the Lord is.
4:
"To whom coming, as unto a living stone, disallowed indeed of men, but chosen of God, and precious," The witnesses note Christ the living stone, rejected by men but chosen of God. Gill notes Christ is here, as often, compared to a "stone"; and Peter, by this metaphor, shows that he is not the rock, but Christ is the rock on which the church is built, the foundation stone on which every believer is laid. The witnesses note Peter describing Christ as a living stone, to whom they come, disallowed of men but chosen of God and precious; the coming to Christ the living stone, rejected by men, prized by God — that Peter sets Christ before them under the figure of a stone: "to whom coming" (drawing near to Christ by faith) "as unto a living stone" (a stone, yet alive — for the foundation of the church is no dead rock but the living Christ, who has life in himself and gives it) — and he marks the double estimate of this stone: "disallowed indeed of men" (rejected and cast aside by men, as the builders refused him) "but chosen of God, and precious"; the witnesses note the contrast that runs through the passage — what men despised, God chose and prized as precious; so that the believer comes to the very stone the world rejected, finding in him the living and chosen foundation that God himself esteems; to whom coming, as unto a living stone, disallowed indeed of men, but chosen of God, and precious. The witnesses note to whom coming, as unto a living stone. The witnesses note disallowed indeed of men, but chosen of God, and precious.

The witnesses note Christ the living stone — alive and life-giving — rejected by men but chosen of God and precious; the believer coming to the very stone the world rejected.

So the witnesses make the verse the coming to Christ the living stone. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers come by faith to Christ, the living stone rejected by men but chosen and precious to God. The witnesses note the coming: "to whom coming" — Gill notes drawing near to Christ by faith; the coming; the by faith; the drawing near; the to Christ; the the stone. The expositors observe the living stone: "as unto a living stone" — a stone, yet alive, the living Christ; the living stone; the alive; the the living Christ; the life in himself; the gives life. The witnesses press the disallowed of men: "disallowed indeed of men" — rejected and cast aside by men; the disallowed of men; the rejected; the cast aside; the the builders; the refused. The expositors draw the lesson on the chosen of God: "but chosen of God, and precious" — chosen and prized by God; the chosen of God; the chosen; the precious; the prized; the esteemed. The witnesses observe the the contrast: what men despised, God chose; the the contrast; the men despised; the God chose; the prized; the precious. The expositors note the the living foundation: the believer finds the living, chosen foundation God esteems; the the living foundation; the chosen; the God esteems; the the believer; the comes. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to whom coming as unto a living stone, the disallowed indeed of men but chosen of God and precious, and the coming to Christ. The verse thus records the coming to Christ the living stone — teaching that believers come by faith to Christ, rejected by men but chosen and precious to God.
5:
"Ye also, as lively stones, are built up a spiritual house, an holy priesthood, to offer up spiritual sacrifices, acceptable to God by Jesus Christ." The witnesses note believers as living stones built into a spiritual house and holy priesthood. Gill notes saints are compared to stones; they lie in the same quarry, and are the same by nature as the rest of mankind, till dug out and separated by the grace of God, then hewn and made fit for the spiritual building. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they also, as living stones, are built up a spiritual house and holy priesthood to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God by Christ; the believers as living stones, a spiritual temple and priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices — that Peter extends the figure from Christ to his people: "ye also, as lively stones" (made living by union with the living Stone, Christ) "are built up a spiritual house" (the church, a living temple of God, raised on Christ the foundation) "an holy priesthood" (every believer a priest, not of one tribe only) "to offer up spiritual sacrifices, acceptable to God by Jesus Christ"; the witnesses note the rich truth — believers are both the temple and its priests, offering not animal sacrifices but "spiritual" ones (praise, prayer, thanksgiving, a broken heart, good works, themselves), and these are accepted by God only "by Jesus Christ," through whom alone all worship is made pleasing; ye also, as lively stones, are built up a spiritual house, an holy priesthood. The witnesses note ye also, as lively stones, are built up a spiritual house, an holy priesthood. The witnesses note to offer up spiritual sacrifices, acceptable to God by Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note believers as living stones built into a spiritual house and holy priesthood — offering spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God only through Christ; both temple and priests.

So the witnesses make the verse the believers as a spiritual temple and priesthood. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers, living stones, are built into a spiritual house and are priests offering spiritual sacrifices through Christ. The witnesses note the lively stones: "ye also, as lively stones" — Gill notes made living by union with Christ; the lively stones; the living; the union with Christ; the the living Stone; the made. The expositors observe the spiritual house: "are built up a spiritual house" — the church, a living temple; the spiritual house; the the church; the the temple; the on Christ; the raised. The witnesses press the holy priesthood: "an holy priesthood" — every believer a priest; the holy priesthood; the every believer; the a priest; the not one tribe; the all. The expositors draw the lesson on the spiritual sacrifices: "to offer up spiritual sacrifices" — praise, prayer, good works, themselves; the spiritual sacrifices; the praise; the prayer; the good works; the themselves. The witnesses observe the by Jesus Christ: "acceptable to God by Jesus Christ" — accepted only through Christ; the by Jesus Christ; the accepted; the through Christ; the pleasing; the alone. The expositors note the temple and priests: believers are both temple and priests; the temple and priests; the both; the the temple; the the priests; the offer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye also as lively stones are built up a spiritual house an holy priesthood, the to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God by Jesus Christ, and the spiritual temple. The verse thus records the believers as a spiritual temple and priesthood — teaching that believers are built into a spiritual house and are priests offering spiritual sacrifices through Christ.
6:
"Wherefore also it is contained in the scripture, Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone, elect, precious: and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded." The witnesses note the scriptural proof of Christ the chief corner stone. Gill notes this is produced as a proof of the excellency of Christ as a stone, his usefulness in the spiritual building, his being chosen of God and precious though rejected by men, and the safety of those that believe. The witnesses note Peter citing the scripture, "Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone, elect, precious: and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded"; the prophetic warrant for Christ the chosen corner stone and the believer's security — that Peter backs his teaching by Isaiah's prophecy: God himself says, "Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone" (Christ, laid by God as the great corner-stone that binds and upholds the whole building of the church) "elect, precious" (chosen and prized by God, as v.4 said) — and he adds the promise to faith: "and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded" (shall never be put to shame, disappointed, or made ashamed of his trust); the witnesses note the comfort — the one who builds his hope on Christ, God's chosen corner stone, rests on a sure foundation and shall never be confounded; behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone, elect, precious. The witnesses note Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone, elect, precious. The witnesses note and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded.

The witnesses note the prophetic warrant — God lays in Sion a chief corner stone, elect and precious; and he that believeth on him shall never be confounded, resting on a sure foundation.

So the witnesses make the verse the scriptural proof of Christ the corner stone. The lesson the expositors draw is that God laid Christ as the chosen corner stone, and the one who believes on him shall never be put to shame. The witnesses note the it is contained: "it is contained in the scripture" — Gill notes the proof from Isaiah; the it is contained; the the scripture; the Isaiah; the the proof; the prophecy. The expositors observe the corner stone: "Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone" — Christ, the great corner-stone; the corner stone; the in Sion; the the church; the binds; the upholds. The witnesses press the elect, precious: "elect, precious" — chosen and prized by God; the elect, precious; the chosen; the prized; the by God; the precious. The expositors draw the lesson on the believeth: "and he that believeth on him" — the one who believes on Christ; the believeth; the on him; the the believer; the trusts; the faith. The witnesses observe the not confounded: "shall not be confounded" — shall never be put to shame; the not confounded; the no shame; the not disappointed; the not ashamed; the secure. The expositors note the sure foundation: he rests on a sure foundation; the sure foundation; the rests; the sure; the Christ; the secure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Behold I lay in Sion a chief corner stone elect precious, the and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded, and the corner stone. The verse thus records the scriptural proof of Christ the corner stone — teaching that God laid Christ as the chosen corner stone, and the one who believes shall never be put to shame.
7:
"Unto you therefore which believe he is precious: but unto them which be disobedient, the stone which the builders disallowed, the same is made the head of the corner," The witnesses note the twofold response to Christ — precious to believers, rejected by the disobedient. Gill notes those who believe are not all that can say their creed, or assent to a divine revelation, but such as have a true and spiritual faith, to whom Christ is precious. The witnesses note Peter declaring that to those who believe Christ is precious, but to the disobedient the rejected stone is made the head of the corner; the dividing of men by their response to the precious yet rejected stone — that Peter shows how Christ divides mankind: "unto you therefore which believe he is precious" (to true believers Christ is altogether precious, prized above all, as he is to God) — "but unto them which be disobedient" (the unbelieving and disobedient) "the stone which the builders disallowed, the same is made the head of the corner"; the witnesses note the solemn truth — the very stone that men, the builders, rejected and cast away, God has exalted to be "the head of the corner," the chief place in the building; so that Christ, despised by the disobedient, is set by God in the highest honour, and is precious to all who believe; unto you therefore which believe he is precious. The witnesses note unto you therefore which believe he is precious. The witnesses note the stone which the builders disallowed, the same is made the head of the corner.

The witnesses note Christ dividing mankind — precious to believers, rejected by the disobedient; the very stone the builders cast away God exalts to be the head of the corner.

So the witnesses make the verse the twofold response to the precious yet rejected stone. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ is precious to believers, while the disobedient reject the stone God has made the head of the corner. The witnesses note the precious to believers: "unto you therefore which believe he is precious" — Gill notes to true believers Christ is precious; the precious to believers; the true believers; the prized; the above all; the precious. The expositors observe the true faith: those who believe have a true, spiritual faith; the true faith; the spiritual; the not mere assent; the the believers; the genuine. The witnesses press the the disobedient: "but unto them which be disobedient" — the unbelieving and disobedient; the the disobedient; the unbelieving; the reject; the cast away; the refuse. The expositors draw the lesson on the disallowed: "the stone which the builders disallowed" — rejected and cast away by men; the disallowed; the rejected; the cast away; the the builders; the refused. The witnesses observe the head of the corner: "the same is made the head of the corner" — God exalts it to the chief place; the head of the corner; the the chief place; the exalted; the by God; the the highest. The expositors note the divides mankind: Christ divides mankind by their response; the divides mankind; the the response; the believers; the disobedient; the divides. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto you therefore which believe he is precious, the the stone which the builders disallowed the same is made the head of the corner, and the twofold response. The verse thus records the twofold response to the precious yet rejected stone — teaching that Christ is precious to believers, while the disobedient reject the stone God has made the head of the corner.
8:
"And a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence, even to them which stumble at the word, being disobedient: whereunto also they were appointed." The witnesses note that Christ is a stumbling-stone to the disobedient. Gill notes the apostle alludes to the prophecy of the Messiah, which had its accomplishment in the unbelieving and disobedient Jews, who stumbled at his birth, his meanness, and his doctrine. The witnesses note Peter declaring that Christ is a stone of stumbling and rock of offence to the disobedient who stumble at the word, to which they were appointed; Christ a stumbling-stone to the disobedient, who were appointed to it — that Peter completes the contrast: the same Christ who is precious to believers is, to the unbelieving, "a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence" — they stumble and fall over him "even to them which stumble at the word, being disobedient" (their stumbling springing from their disobedience to the gospel); and Peter adds a solemn word: "whereunto also they were appointed"; the witnesses note the debate on this last clause — that the disobedient were "appointed" (in God's righteous, sovereign ordination) to this stumbling and its consequence, the Reformed taking it of God's just judicial appointment of the reprobate to the ruin their unbelief deserves, while all agree the stumbling itself is the fruit of their own disobedience; whereunto also they were appointed. The witnesses note a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence. The witnesses note even to them which stumble at the word, being disobedient: whereunto also they were appointed.

The witnesses note Christ a stumbling-stone to the disobedient — who stumble at the word through their unbelief; and the solemn clause that they "were appointed" to it, debated as God's judicial ordination.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ a stumbling-stone to the disobedient. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disobedient stumble at Christ through their unbelief, to which they were appointed. The witnesses note the stone of stumbling: "a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence" — Gill notes the disobedient stumble over Christ; the stone of stumbling; the rock of offence; the stumble; the over Christ; the fall. The expositors observe the the disobedient Jews: fulfilled in the unbelieving Jews who stumbled at his meanness; the the disobedient Jews; the unbelieving; the stumbled; the his meanness; the his doctrine. The witnesses press the stumble at the word: "even to them which stumble at the word, being disobedient" — stumbling springing from disobedience; the stumble at the word; the disobedient; the their unbelief; the the gospel; the the cause. The expositors present the debate: the witnesses note the debate on "whereunto also they were appointed" — the Reformed take it of God's just judicial appointment of the reprobate to the ruin their unbelief deserves; all agree the stumbling is the fruit of their own disobedience. The witnesses observe the appointed: "whereunto also they were appointed" — appointed in God's sovereign ordination; the appointed; the God's ordination; the sovereign; the the judgment; the just. The expositors note the their own disobedience: the stumbling itself is the fruit of their disobedience; the their own disobedience; the the fruit; the the stumbling; the unbelief; the their own. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a stone of stumbling and a rock of offence, the even to them which stumble at the word being disobedient whereunto also they were appointed, and Christ the stumbling-stone. The verse thus records Christ a stumbling-stone to the disobedient — teaching that the disobedient stumble at Christ through their unbelief, to which they were appointed.
9:
"But ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people; that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light:" The witnesses note the glorious titles of the people of God. Gill notes the allusion throughout is to the people of Israel, who in an external way were all that is here said; the believers are this in a spiritual sense — a chosen, royal, holy, and peculiar people. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a peculiar people, to show forth the praises of him who called them from darkness to light; the high titles of the church and the purpose of their calling — that Peter, against the dark lot of the disobedient (v.8), heaps up the glorious titles of believers: "a chosen generation" (a race chosen by God), "a royal priesthood" (kings and priests unto God), "an holy nation" (a people set apart and sanctified), "a peculiar people" (God's own special possession, a people for himself) — and he names the great end of all this dignity: "that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light"; the witnesses note that the believers' high standing is for a purpose — to proclaim and display the excellencies of the God who called them out of the darkness of sin and ignorance into the marvellous light of his grace and truth; that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light. The witnesses note but ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people. The witnesses note that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light.

The witnesses note the glorious titles of the church — a chosen generation, royal priesthood, holy nation, peculiar people — called from darkness to light to show forth God's praises.

So the witnesses make the verse the high titles of the church and their purpose. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers are chosen, royal, holy, and God's own, that they might proclaim his praises, called from darkness to light. The witnesses note the chosen generation: "a chosen generation" — Gill notes a race chosen by God; the chosen generation; the chosen; the by God; the a race; the elect. The expositors observe the royal priesthood: "a royal priesthood" — kings and priests to God; the royal priesthood; the kings; the priests; the to God; the royal. The witnesses press the holy nation: "an holy nation, a peculiar people" — set apart, God's own possession; the holy nation; the set apart; the peculiar people; the God's own; the possession. The expositors draw the lesson on the shew forth: "that ye should shew forth the praises" — to proclaim God's excellencies; the shew forth; the the praises; the proclaim; the the excellencies; the display. The witnesses observe the from darkness to light: "called you out of darkness into his marvellous light" — from sin's darkness to grace's light; the darkness to light; the sin's darkness; the marvellous light; the grace; the called. The expositors note the the purpose: their high standing is for the purpose of praising God; the the purpose; the praising God; the proclaim; the display. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light, and the high titles. The verse thus records the high titles of the church and their purpose — teaching that believers are chosen, royal, holy, and God's own, that they might proclaim his praises.
10:
"Which in time past were not a people, but are now the people of God: which had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy." The witnesses note the change from being no people to the people of God. Gill notes God's elect, whether among Jews or Gentiles, were from eternity his chosen and covenant people, given to Christ; but in time past, before conversion, they were not a people, having a "Loammi" put upon them. The witnesses note Peter declaring that in time past they were not a people, but are now the people of God, and had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy; the great change from being no people to God's people through mercy — that Peter, recalling Hosea's prophecy, marks the wonderful change grace has wrought: "which in time past were not a people" (before, they were no people of God, outside his covenant, especially the Gentiles) "but are now the people of God" (now openly owned and made his people) — "which had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy"; the witnesses note the double turn — from "not a people" to "the people of God," and from "not obtained mercy" to "obtained mercy"; so that their whole new standing rests on the free mercy of God, who took those who were no people and not pitied, and made them his own and the objects of his mercy; which had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy. The witnesses note which in time past were not a people, but are now the people of God. The witnesses note which had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy.

The witnesses note the great change wrought by grace — from being no people to the people of God, from not pitied to obtaining mercy; their whole new standing resting on God's free mercy.

So the witnesses make the verse the change from no people to God's people through mercy. The lesson the expositors draw is that grace took those who were no people and not pitied, and made them God's own, objects of his mercy. The witnesses note the not a people: "which in time past were not a people" — Gill notes before conversion, no people of God; the not a people; the before; the no people; the outside; the the covenant. The expositors observe the the Gentiles: especially the Gentiles, outside the covenant; the the Gentiles; the outside; the the covenant; the no people; the strangers. The witnesses press the the people of God: "but are now the people of God" — now openly made his people; the the people of God; the now; the openly; the made his; the owned. The expositors draw the lesson on the not obtained mercy: "which had not obtained mercy" — before, not pitied; the not obtained mercy; the not pitied; the before; the without; the no mercy. The witnesses observe the now have mercy: "but now have obtained mercy" — now the objects of his mercy; the now have mercy; the obtained; the objects; the his mercy; the pitied. The expositors note the rests on mercy: their whole new standing rests on God's free mercy; the rests on mercy; the free mercy; the God's; the new standing; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which in time past were not a people but are now the people of God, the which had not obtained mercy but now have obtained mercy, and the change by mercy. The verse thus records the change from no people to God's people through mercy — teaching that grace took those who were no people and made them God's own, objects of his mercy.
11:
"Dearly beloved, I beseech you as strangers and pilgrims, abstain from fleshly lusts, which war against the soul;" The witnesses note the appeal to pilgrims to abstain from fleshly lusts. Gill notes the apostle, from characters expressing the saints' blessings and privileges, passes to exhortations to duties; he addresses them as "dearly beloved" to express his great affection. The witnesses note Peter beseeching them, as strangers and pilgrims, to abstain from fleshly lusts which war against the soul; the pilgrim's call to abstain from the soul-warring lusts of the flesh — that Peter, turning from privilege to duty, makes a tender appeal grounded in their pilgrim character: "dearly beloved, I beseech you as strangers and pilgrims" (as those who are but sojourners here, not citizens of this world, their home being heaven) "abstain from fleshly lusts" (keep wholly away from the sinful desires of the flesh) "which war against the soul"; the witnesses note the reason that gives weight to the appeal — these fleshly lusts are not harmless indulgences but enemies that "war against the soul," waging a deadly campaign against the believer's spiritual life and welfare; so that the pilgrim, bound for a better country, must hold himself apart from the lusts that fight against his very soul; abstain from fleshly lusts, which war against the soul. The witnesses note I beseech you as strangers and pilgrims, abstain from fleshly lusts. The witnesses note which war against the soul.

The witnesses note the pilgrim's call to abstain from fleshly lusts — not harmless indulgences but enemies that war against the soul; the sojourner holding apart from what fights his spiritual life.

So the witnesses make the verse the pilgrim's abstaining from soul-warring lusts. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer, a stranger and pilgrim, is to abstain from the fleshly lusts that war against his soul. The witnesses note the beseech: "dearly beloved, I beseech you" — Gill notes a tender appeal of great affection; the beseech; the tender; the affection; the dearly beloved; the appeal. The expositors observe the strangers and pilgrims: "as strangers and pilgrims" — sojourners here, home in heaven; the strangers and pilgrims; the sojourners; the not citizens; the home; the heaven. The witnesses press the abstain: "abstain from fleshly lusts" — keep wholly away from sinful desire; the abstain; the keep away; the fleshly lusts; the sinful desire; the apart. The expositors draw the lesson on the war against the soul: "which war against the soul" — enemies waging war on the soul; the war against the soul; the enemies; the deadly; the the soul; the campaign. The witnesses observe the not harmless: these lusts are not harmless indulgences but enemies; the not harmless; the not indulgences; the enemies; the deadly; the fight. The expositors note the spiritual life: they war against the believer's spiritual life and welfare; the spiritual life; the welfare; the the soul; the fight; the enemies. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I beseech you as strangers and pilgrims abstain from fleshly lusts, the which war against the soul, and the pilgrim's call. The verse thus records the pilgrim's abstaining from soul-warring lusts — teaching that the believer, a stranger and pilgrim, is to abstain from the fleshly lusts that war against his soul.
12:
"Having your conversation honest among the Gentiles: that, whereas they speak against you as evildoers, they may by your good works, which they shall behold, glorify God in the day of visitation." The witnesses note the call to an honest life that wins the heathen to glorify God. Gill notes to have the conversation honest is to live and walk honestly before all, to do the things which are right and honest in the sight of God and men, and to order the conversation aright. The witnesses note Peter charging that their conduct among the Gentiles be honest, that the heathen, who speak against them as evildoers, may by their good works glorify God in the day of visitation; the honest life that silences slander and wins the heathen to glorify God — that Peter bids the believers, living among unbelieving Gentiles, keep "your conversation honest" (a manner of life upright, honourable, and good before all) — and he names the gracious effect this may have: "that, whereas they speak against you as evildoers" (though the heathen slander and accuse the Christians falsely) "they may by your good works, which they shall behold, glorify God in the day of visitation"; the witnesses note the hope held out — that the very heathen who now revile them, seeing the believers' good and honourable works, may be brought to acknowledge and glorify God when his grace visits them; so that a holy, honest life is a witness that may turn slanderers into worshippers; that they may by your good works, which they shall behold, glorify God in the day of visitation. The witnesses note having your conversation honest among the Gentiles. The witnesses note that they may by your good works, which they shall behold, glorify God in the day of visitation.

The witnesses note the honest life that silences slander — that the heathen who now revile them, seeing their good works, may glorify God in the day of visitation; a witness turning slanderers to worshippers.

So the witnesses make the verse the honest life that wins the heathen to glorify God. The lesson the expositors draw is that an upright life among unbelievers may turn their slander into the glorifying of God. The witnesses note the honest: "having your conversation honest among the Gentiles" — Gill notes an upright, honourable life before all; the honest; the upright; the honourable; the before all; the good. The expositors observe the speak against you: "whereas they speak against you as evildoers" — the heathen slander them falsely; the speak against you; the slander; the falsely; the evildoers; the accuse. The witnesses press the good works: "they may by your good works, which they shall behold" — seeing the believers' good works; the good works; the beheld; the seen; the the believers; the good. The expositors draw the lesson on the glorify God: "glorify God in the day of visitation" — brought to glorify God; the glorify God; the brought; the acknowledge; the the day of visitation; the grace visits. The witnesses observe the silences slander: the honest life silences slander; the silences slander; the the honest life; the silences; the witness; the slander. The expositors note the slanderers to worshippers: a witness that may turn slanderers into worshippers; the slanderers to worshippers; the turn; the the witness; the glorify; the converted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the having your conversation honest among the Gentiles, the that they may by your good works which they shall behold glorify God in the day of visitation, and the honest life. The verse thus records the honest life that wins the heathen to glorify God — teaching that an upright life among unbelievers may turn their slander into the glorifying of God.
13:
"Submit yourselves to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake: whether it be to the king, as supreme;" The witnesses note the charge to submit to human authority for the Lord's sake. Gill notes "every ordinance of man" is "every human creation" or institution; not all individuals, for some are not to be submitted to, but the civil ordinances and authorities appointed among men. The witnesses note Peter charging that they submit to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake, whether to the king as supreme; the duty of submission to civil authority out of obedience to the Lord — that Peter turns to the believers' duty toward the state: "submit yourselves to every ordinance of man" (yield obedience to the lawful institutions and authorities of human government) "for the Lord's sake" (and that from a right motive — not from fear or constraint only, but out of obedience and reverence to the Lord, who has ordained government) — "whether it be to the king, as supreme"; the witnesses note the breadth and ground of the duty — submission is due to the whole order of civil authority, beginning with the highest, "the king, as supreme," and it is rendered "for the Lord's sake," so that obedience to lawful magistrates is part of the Christian's obedience to God himself; submit yourselves to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake. The witnesses note submit yourselves to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake. The witnesses note whether it be to the king, as supreme.

The witnesses note the duty of submission to civil authority for the Lord's sake — beginning with the king as supreme; obedience to lawful magistrates part of the Christian's obedience to God.

So the witnesses make the verse submission to civil authority for the Lord's sake. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer submits to lawful human government out of obedience to the Lord who ordained it. The witnesses note the submit: "submit yourselves to every ordinance of man" — Gill notes yield to lawful human institutions; the submit; the every ordinance; the human institutions; the lawful; the authorities. The expositors observe the not all individuals: "every ordinance of man" is the institutions, not all individuals; the not all individuals; the the institutions; the the authorities; the civil; the lawful. The witnesses press the for the Lord's sake: "for the Lord's sake" — from obedience and reverence to the Lord; the for the Lord's sake; the obedience; the reverence; the the Lord; the the motive. The expositors draw the lesson on the the king: "whether it be to the king, as supreme" — beginning with the highest, the king; the the king; the supreme; the the highest; the beginning; the authority. The witnesses observe the the breadth: submission is due to the whole order of authority; the the breadth; the the whole order; the authority; the submission; the due. The expositors note the obedience to God: obedience to magistrates is part of obedience to God; the obedience to God; the to magistrates; the part of; the the Christian; the God himself. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the submit yourselves to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake, the whether it be to the king as supreme, and the submission to authority. The verse thus records submission to civil authority for the Lord's sake — teaching that the believer submits to lawful human government out of obedience to the Lord who ordained it.
14:
"Or unto governors, as unto them that are sent by him for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well." The witnesses note submission to subordinate governors and the purpose of their office. Gill notes "governors" are inferior magistrates under the Roman emperor, such as proconsuls and procurators, like Pontius Pilate, Felix, and Festus, who governed particular provinces under the emperor. The witnesses note Peter directing submission also to governors, sent for the punishment of evildoers and the praise of those who do well; the submission due to magistrates and the God-given purpose of government — that Peter, having named the king (v.13), extends submission to the lower ranks: "or unto governors" (the subordinate officers under the supreme power) — and he gives the divine purpose of all such authority: "as unto them that are sent by him for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well"; the witnesses note the twofold end of government as God ordained it — to restrain and punish the wicked ("the punishment of evildoers") and to encourage and commend the good ("the praise of them that do well"); so that, since rulers are sent for this good and God-appointed end, the believer is rightly to submit to them, doing well and so winning their approval rather than their punishment; sent by him for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well. The witnesses note or unto governors, as unto them that are sent by him. The witnesses note for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well.

The witnesses note submission to governors and the God-given purpose of government — to punish evildoers and praise those who do well; the believer submitting by doing well and winning approval.

So the witnesses make the verse submission to magistrates and government's purpose. The lesson the expositors draw is that government is sent by God to punish the wicked and commend the good, so the believer submits by doing well. The witnesses note the governors: "or unto governors" — Gill notes inferior magistrates under the emperor; the governors; the inferior magistrates; the under the emperor; the proconsuls; the subordinate. The expositors observe the sent by him: "as unto them that are sent by him" — sent by the king and ultimately by God; the sent by him; the the king; the by God; the appointed; the authority. The witnesses press the punishment of evildoers: "for the punishment of evildoers" — to restrain and punish the wicked; the punishment of evildoers; the restrain; the punish; the the wicked; the curb. The expositors draw the lesson on the praise of them that do well: "and for the praise of them that do well" — to encourage and commend the good; the praise of them that do well; the encourage; the commend; the the good; the reward. The witnesses observe the twofold end: the twofold end of government as God ordained it; the twofold end; the God ordained; the punish; the praise; the the end. The expositors note the submit by doing well: the believer submits by doing well, winning approval; the submit by doing well; the doing well; the approval; the not punishment; the submits. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the or unto governors as unto them that are sent by him, the for the punishment of evildoers and for the praise of them that do well, and the purpose of government. The verse thus records submission to magistrates and government's purpose — teaching that government is sent by God to punish the wicked and commend the good, so the believer submits by doing well.
15:
"For so is the will of God, that with well doing ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men:" The witnesses note that well-doing silences the ignorance of foolish men. Gill notes "so is the will of God" refers chiefly to what follows: that with well doing they may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men. Barnes notes that it is the will of God that by upright conduct his people should silence calumny. The witnesses note Peter declaring that it is the will of God that by well doing they put to silence the ignorance of foolish men; the silencing of slander by a life of well-doing, which is God's will — that Peter gives the divine reason for the believers' good and submissive conduct: "for so is the will of God" (this is what God wills and requires) "that with well doing" (by a life of doing good and right) "ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men"; the witnesses note the gracious strategy — the believers' answer to slander is not retaliation in words but a manner of life so upright that it stops the mouths of their accusers; the "foolish men" who, in their "ignorance," speak against the Christians are best refuted and silenced not by argument but by good works that leave nothing to be said against them; so that a holy life is God's appointed way to silence ungodly slander; that with well doing ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men. The witnesses note for so is the will of God. The witnesses note that with well doing ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men.

The witnesses note that well-doing silences the ignorance of foolish men — God's appointed way; the answer to slander not retaliation but a life so upright it stops the accusers' mouths.

So the witnesses make the verse the silencing of slander by well-doing. The lesson the expositors draw is that God wills his people to silence the ignorant slander of foolish men by a life of well-doing. The witnesses note the will of God: "for so is the will of God" — Gill notes this is what God wills; the will of God; the requires; the wills; the divine; the appointed. The expositors observe the with well doing: "that with well doing" — by a life of doing good; the with well doing; the doing good; the right; the upright; the the life. The witnesses press the put to silence: "ye may put to silence" — stop the mouths of accusers; the put to silence; the stop; the the mouths; the the accusers; the refute. The expositors draw the lesson on the ignorance: "the ignorance of foolish men" — the ignorant slander of fools; the ignorance; the foolish men; the the slander; the ignorant; the the fools. The witnesses observe the not retaliation: the answer is not retaliation but upright living; the not retaliation; the not words; the upright living; the the answer; the good works. The expositors note the God's way: a holy life is God's appointed way to silence slander; the God's way; the a holy life; the appointed; the silence; the slander. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for so is the will of God, the that with well doing ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men, and the silencing of slander. The verse thus records the silencing of slander by well-doing — teaching that God wills his people to silence the ignorant slander of foolish men by a life of well-doing.
16:
"As free, and not using your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness, but as the servants of God." The witnesses note the right use of Christian liberty. Gill notes these converted Jews might value themselves on their freedom, as descendants of Abraham and freeborn, and chiefly because of the liberty they had by Christ; the apostle allows they were freemen, but warns of the abuse of it. The witnesses note Peter charging that, as free, they not use their liberty as a cloak of maliciousness, but as the servants of God; the true freedom that does not abuse liberty but serves God — that Peter guards against a misuse of Christian liberty in the matter of submission: the believers are indeed "free" (made free by Christ, not slaves of men or of the law) — yet they are "not [to use] your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness" (not to make their freedom a pretext or cover for doing evil, disorder, or shaking off lawful authority) "but as the servants of God"; the witnesses note the paradox of gospel freedom — the believer is free, yet a servant of God, and his very liberty is to be used in willing obedience to God, not as an excuse for license; so that true Christian freedom never cloaks sin or rebellion, but expresses itself in glad service to God; not using your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness, but as the servants of God. The witnesses note as free, and not using your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness. The witnesses note but as the servants of God.

The witnesses note the right use of Christian liberty — free, yet not using freedom as a cloak for evil, but as God's servants; true freedom expressed in glad service, never cloaking sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the true use of Christian liberty. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is free, yet uses his freedom not as a cover for evil but in willing service to God. The witnesses note the as free: "as free" — Gill notes made free by Christ, not slaves; the as free; the made free; the by Christ; the not slaves; the freemen. The expositors observe the not a cloke: "not using your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness" — not a cover for evil; the not a cloke; the not a cover; the maliciousness; the evil; the pretext. The witnesses press the not disorder: not a pretext for disorder or shaking off authority; the not disorder; the not shaking off; the authority; the license; the pretext. The expositors draw the lesson on the servants of God: "but as the servants of God" — free, yet servants of God; the servants of God; the free yet servants; the obedience; the willing; the service. The witnesses observe the the paradox: the paradox of gospel freedom — free, yet a servant; the the paradox; the gospel freedom; the free; the a servant; the both. The expositors note the never cloaks sin: true freedom never cloaks sin or rebellion; the never cloaks sin; the not rebellion; the true freedom; the glad service; the obedience. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as free and not using your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness, the but as the servants of God, and the right use of liberty. The verse thus records the true use of Christian liberty — teaching that the believer is free, yet uses his freedom not as a cover for evil but in willing service to God.
17:
"Honour all men. Love the brotherhood. Fear God. Honour the king." The witnesses note the fourfold rule of Christian duty. Gill notes "honour all men" means giving honour to whom honour is due, according to their place, station, circumstances, and usefulness, whether Jews or Gentiles, rich or poor, believers or unbelievers. The witnesses note Peter charging them to honour all men, love the brotherhood, fear God, and honour the king; the fourfold compass of Christian duty toward all, the saints, God, and the king — that Peter sums up the believer's social and religious duties in four brief, pointed commands: "honour all men" (render to every man the respect due to him as a fellow-creature, made in God's image), "love the brotherhood" (a special, warm love to the whole family of believers), "fear God" (a reverent, holy fear and worship of God above all), and "honour the king" (give due respect to the civil ruler); the witnesses note the order and proportion — honour is due to all men, but love to the brethren, fear to God alone, and honour to the king; so that each receives its proper measure — general honour to all, special love to the saints, supreme fear to God, and civil honour to the ruler; honour all men. Love the brotherhood. Fear God. Honour the king. The witnesses note honour all men. Love the brotherhood. The witnesses note Fear God. Honour the king.

The witnesses note the fourfold rule — honour all men, love the brotherhood, fear God, honour the king; each receiving its proper measure: general honour, special love, supreme fear, civil honour.

So the witnesses make the verse the fourfold compass of Christian duty. The lesson the expositors draw is that honour is due to all, special love to the brethren, fear to God alone, and honour to the king. The witnesses note the honour all men: "honour all men" — Gill notes honour due to every man in his place; the honour all men; the every man; the due respect; the fellow-creature; the God's image. The expositors observe the love the brotherhood: "Love the brotherhood" — special, warm love to believers; the love the brotherhood; the special love; the warm; the the family; the believers. The witnesses press the fear God: "Fear God" — reverent, holy fear of God above all; the fear God; the reverent; the holy fear; the worship; the above all. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour the king: "Honour the king" — due respect to the civil ruler; the honour the king; the due respect; the the ruler; the civil; the honour. The witnesses observe the the proportion: each receives its proper measure; the the proportion; the proper measure; the each; the order; the proportion. The expositors note the the four: general honour, special love, supreme fear, civil honour; the the four; the general honour; the special love; the supreme fear; the civil honour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the honour all men love the brotherhood, the Fear God Honour the king, and the fourfold rule. The verse thus records the fourfold compass of Christian duty — teaching that honour is due to all, special love to the brethren, fear to God alone, and honour to the king.
18:
"Servants, be subject to your masters with all fear; not only to the good and gentle, but also to the froward." The witnesses note the duty of servants to be subject even to harsh masters. Gill notes some Jews held that, being Abraham's seed, they ought not to be the servants of any; and some believers thought they ought not to serve unbelieving masters, nor even believing ones; against this Peter charges submission. The witnesses note Peter charging servants to be subject to their masters with all fear, not only the good and gentle but also the froward; the submission of servants required even toward harsh and unjust masters — that Peter turns to the duty of Christian servants (a large class then): "servants, be subject to your masters with all fear" (yield a dutiful, reverent obedience to those over them) — and he extends it to the hardest case: "not only to the good and gentle" (the kind and reasonable masters, easy to serve) "but also to the froward" (the harsh, perverse, and unjust ones); the witnesses note the searching demand — Christian submission is not to be measured by the master's character, withheld from the cruel and given only to the kind, but rendered even to the difficult and unfair master, for conscience toward God; so that the servant's duty stands firm whatever the master's temper; not only to the good and gentle, but also to the froward. The witnesses note servants, be subject to your masters with all fear. The witnesses note not only to the good and gentle, but also to the froward.

The witnesses note the submission of servants required even toward harsh masters — not only the gentle but the froward; submission not measured by the master's character but rendered for conscience.

So the witnesses make the verse the submission of servants even to harsh masters. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christian servants are to submit not only to kind but to harsh and unjust masters, for conscience toward God. The witnesses note the be subject: "servants, be subject to your masters with all fear" — Gill notes dutiful, reverent obedience; the be subject; the dutiful; the reverent; the obedience; the with fear. The expositors observe the against the notion: against the notion that they need not serve; the against the notion; the Abraham's seed; the need not serve; the unbelieving masters; the charged. The witnesses press the good and gentle: "not only to the good and gentle" — the kind, easy masters; the good and gentle; the kind; the reasonable; the easy; the to serve. The expositors draw the lesson on the the froward: "but also to the froward" — the harsh, perverse, unjust ones; the the froward; the harsh; the perverse; the unjust; the difficult. The witnesses observe the not measured by character: submission is not measured by the master's character; the not measured by character; the the master; the not withheld; the the cruel; the rendered. The expositors note the for conscience: rendered for conscience toward God; the for conscience; the toward God; the the servant; the firm; the duty. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the servants be subject to your masters with all fear, the not only to the good and gentle but also to the froward, and the submission of servants. The verse thus records the submission of servants even to harsh masters — teaching that Christian servants are to submit not only to kind but to harsh masters, for conscience toward God.
19:
"For this is thankworthy, if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully." The witnesses note that patient suffering of wrong for conscience' sake is acceptable to God. Gill notes "this is thankworthy," or "grace," a fruit and effect of grace, an instance of it; the Alexandrian copy adds "with God," that is, this is grateful and acceptable to God. The witnesses note Peter declaring that it is thankworthy if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully; the grace of patiently bearing unjust suffering for God's sake — that Peter gives the principle that ennobles the servant's hard lot: "for this is thankworthy" (this is a thing of grace, acceptable and pleasing to God) "if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully"; the witnesses note the two marks that make such suffering acceptable — first, the cause: it must be suffered "wrongfully" (the man being innocent, suffering unjustly, not for his own faults), and second, the motive: "for conscience toward God" (borne out of regard to God and a tender conscience, not mere necessity); so that when a believer endures undeserved grief patiently, out of conscience toward God, it is no mean thing but a grace well-pleasing in God's sight; this is thankworthy, if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully. The witnesses note for this is thankworthy. The witnesses note if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully.

The witnesses note the grace of patiently bearing unjust suffering for God's sake — innocent suffering, borne out of conscience toward God, no mean thing but well-pleasing in his sight.

So the witnesses make the verse the grace of bearing unjust suffering for God. The lesson the expositors draw is that to endure undeserved grief patiently, for conscience toward God, is acceptable and pleasing to him. The witnesses note the thankworthy: "for this is thankworthy" — Gill notes a fruit of grace, acceptable to God; the thankworthy; the a fruit of grace; the acceptable; the pleasing; the to God. The expositors observe the conscience toward God: "if a man for conscience toward God" — borne out of regard to God; the conscience toward God; the regard to God; the the motive; the tender conscience; the not necessity. The witnesses press the endure grief: "endure grief" — patiently bear the grief; the endure grief; the patiently; the bear; the the grief; the suffer. The expositors draw the lesson on the suffering wrongfully: "suffering wrongfully" — innocent, suffering unjustly; the suffering wrongfully; the innocent; the unjustly; the not for faults; the undeserved. The witnesses observe the two marks: the cause (wrongful) and the motive (conscience) make it acceptable; the two marks; the the cause; the the motive; the wrongful; the conscience. The expositors note the no mean thing: such suffering is no mean thing but a grace; the no mean thing; the a grace; the well-pleasing; the in God's sight; the acceptable. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for this is thankworthy, the if a man for conscience toward God endure grief suffering wrongfully, and the grace of bearing wrong. The verse thus records the grace of bearing unjust suffering for God — teaching that to endure undeserved grief patiently, for conscience toward God, is acceptable and pleasing to him.
20:
"For what glory is it, if, when ye be buffeted for your faults, ye shall take it patiently? but if, when ye do well, and suffer for it, ye take it patiently, this is acceptable with God." The witnesses note the difference between suffering for faults and suffering for well-doing. Barnes notes "what glory is it" — what honour or credit would it be — if, when ye be buffeted for your faults, that is, if you are punished when you deserve it, you take it patiently. The witnesses note Peter asking what glory there is in patiently bearing deserved punishment, but declaring that to suffer patiently for well-doing is acceptable with God; the acceptable patience that bears suffering for well-doing, not for faults — that Peter sharpens the point: there is no special credit in enduring deserved punishment patiently — "for what glory is it, if, when ye be buffeted for your faults, ye shall take it patiently?" (if a man is beaten for real wrongdoing and bears it, that is only what justice requires, no glory) — "but if, when ye do well, and suffer for it, ye take it patiently, this is acceptable with God"; the witnesses note the crucial distinction — patience under deserved chastening is no virtue, but patience under undeserved suffering, when one has done well and yet is made to suffer, is the very thing "acceptable with God," a true grace that pleases him; so that the glory lies not in suffering as such, but in patiently suffering for righteousness; if, when ye do well, and suffer for it, ye take it patiently, this is acceptable with God. The witnesses note for what glory is it, if, when ye be buffeted for your faults, ye shall take it patiently. The witnesses note if, when ye do well, and suffer for it, ye take it patiently, this is acceptable with God.

The witnesses note the distinction — no glory in bearing deserved punishment, but patient suffering for well-doing is acceptable with God; the glory lies in suffering patiently for righteousness.

So the witnesses make the verse the acceptable patience under undeserved suffering. The lesson the expositors draw is that patience under deserved chastening is no virtue, but patience under suffering for well-doing is acceptable with God. The witnesses note the what glory: "for what glory is it" — Barnes notes no honour in bearing deserved punishment; the what glory; the no honour; the no credit; the deserved; the punishment. The expositors observe the buffeted for faults: "if, when ye be buffeted for your faults, ye shall take it patiently" — beaten for real wrongdoing; the buffeted for faults; the real wrongdoing; the deserved; the justice; the no glory. The witnesses press the do well and suffer: "but if, when ye do well, and suffer for it" — suffering though doing well; the do well and suffer; the doing well; the suffering; the undeserved; the innocent. The expositors draw the lesson on the take it patiently: "ye take it patiently" — bearing it patiently; the take it patiently; the bearing; the patiently; the endure; the suffer. The witnesses observe the acceptable with God: "this is acceptable with God" — this pleases God; the acceptable with God; the pleases; the the grace; the well-pleasing; the accepted. The expositors note the glory in righteousness: the glory lies in suffering patiently for righteousness; the glory in righteousness; the for righteousness; the the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if when ye do well and suffer for it ye take it patiently this is acceptable with God, and the acceptable patience. The verse thus records the acceptable patience under undeserved suffering — teaching that patience under deserved chastening is no virtue, but patience under suffering for well-doing is acceptable with God.
21:
"For even hereunto were ye called: because Christ also suffered for us, leaving us an example, that ye should follow his steps:" The witnesses note that believers are called to suffer, with Christ as their example. Gill notes they were called both to well doing, of which the effectually called are capable, and to suffer for so doing, which they must always expect, and to patience in suffering for it. The witnesses note Peter declaring that to this they were called, because Christ also suffered for us, leaving an example that we should follow his steps; the believer's calling to suffer patiently after Christ's own example — that Peter grounds the duty of patient suffering in the believer's very calling and in Christ's example: "for even hereunto were ye called" (Christians are called not only to do well but to suffer for it patiently) "because Christ also suffered for us, leaving us an example, that ye should follow his steps"; the witnesses note the two truths joined here — Christ "suffered for us" (his suffering was first and chiefly redemptive, for our sakes, a thing we cannot imitate) and yet also "leaving us an example" (in the manner of his suffering, his patience and meekness, he set a pattern we are to "follow," treading in his very footsteps); so that the suffering Christian looks to his suffering Lord, both as Saviour and as model; Christ also suffered for us, leaving us an example, that ye should follow his steps. The witnesses note for even hereunto were ye called: because Christ also suffered for us. The witnesses note leaving us an example, that ye should follow his steps.

The witnesses note the believer's calling to suffer patiently after Christ's example — who suffered for us (redemptive) and left us an example in his patience, that we should follow his steps.

So the witnesses make the verse the calling to suffer after Christ's example. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers are called to suffer patiently, Christ having both suffered for them and left an example to follow. The witnesses note the called: "for even hereunto were ye called" — Gill notes called to suffer for well-doing; the called; the to suffer; the for well-doing; the expect; the patience. The expositors observe the suffered for us: "because Christ also suffered for us" — his suffering redemptive, for our sakes; the suffered for us; the redemptive; the for our sakes; the first; the cannot imitate. The witnesses press the an example: "leaving us an example" — in his patience and meekness, a pattern; the an example; the his patience; the meekness; the a pattern; the the manner. The expositors draw the lesson on the follow his steps: "that ye should follow his steps" — tread in his footsteps; the follow his steps; the tread; the his footsteps; the imitate; the the pattern. The witnesses observe the two truths: his suffering both redemptive and exemplary; the two truths; the redemptive; the exemplary; the both; the for us and example. The expositors note the Saviour and model: the Christian looks to Christ as Saviour and model; the Saviour and model; the the Christian; the Saviour; the model; the looks. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for even hereunto were ye called because Christ also suffered for us, the leaving us an example that ye should follow his steps, and the calling to suffer. The verse thus records the calling to suffer after Christ's example — teaching that believers are called to suffer patiently, Christ having both suffered for them and left an example to follow.
22:
"Who did no sin, neither was guile found in his mouth:" The witnesses note the sinless perfection of the suffering Christ. Gill notes Christ was in the likeness of sinful flesh and looked like a sinful man, being born of a woman and keeping company with sinners, and was traduced as a sinner; yet he did no sin. The witnesses note Peter declaring that Christ did no sin, neither was guile found in his mouth; the spotless innocence of Christ amid his unjust suffering — that Peter, setting forth Christ as the pattern of patient suffering, first marks his perfect sinlessness, the ground of his being the supreme example of suffering wrongfully: "who did no sin" (in his whole life he committed not one act of sin, perfectly holy in deed) "neither was guile found in his mouth" (no deceit or falsehood in his words, perfectly pure in speech as in deed); the witnesses note the force for the argument — Christ, though utterly innocent in act and word, suffered the cruellest wrongs; so that the believers, who suffer though sinful, have in their sinless Lord the highest model of innocent suffering patiently borne, and no ground to complain when they suffer who are not wholly innocent as he was; who did no sin, neither was guile found in his mouth. The witnesses note who did no sin. The witnesses note neither was guile found in his mouth.

The witnesses note the spotless innocence of the suffering Christ — who did no sin, no guile in his mouth; the sinless Lord the supreme model of innocent suffering patiently borne.

So the witnesses make the verse the sinless innocence of the suffering Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, perfectly sinless in deed and word, is the supreme example of innocent suffering. The witnesses note the did no sin: "who did no sin" — Gill notes he committed not one act of sin; the did no sin; the not one act; the perfectly holy; the in deed; the sinless. The expositors observe the looked like a sinner: he looked like a sinful man, yet did no sin; the looked like a sinner; the sinful flesh; the traduced; the yet did no sin; the holy. The witnesses press the no guile: "neither was guile found in his mouth" — no deceit in his words; the no guile; the no deceit; the no falsehood; the his words; the pure. The expositors draw the lesson on the pure in speech: perfectly pure in speech as in deed; the pure in speech; the in word; the in deed; the perfectly; the holy. The witnesses observe the suffered wrongs: though innocent, he suffered the cruellest wrongs; the suffered wrongs; the innocent; the the cruellest; the unjust; the suffered. The expositors note the highest model: the sinless Lord is the highest model of innocent suffering; the highest model; the sinless Lord; the innocent suffering; the patiently borne; the the pattern. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who did no sin, the neither was guile found in his mouth, and the sinless innocence. The verse thus records the sinless innocence of the suffering Christ — teaching that Christ, perfectly sinless in deed and word, is the supreme example of innocent suffering.
23:
"Who, when he was reviled, reviled not again; when he suffered, he threatened not; but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously:" The witnesses note Christ's meekness under reviling and his trust in the righteous Judge. Gill notes when Christ was reproached as a glutton, a winebibber, a friend of sinners, and charged with casting out devils by Beelzebub, he defended himself meekly and made no railing reply. The witnesses note Peter declaring that Christ, when reviled, reviled not again, and when he suffered, threatened not, but committed himself to him that judges righteously; the perfect meekness of Christ, who answered wrong not with revenge but with trust in God — that Peter draws out the manner of Christ's suffering as the pattern for the believer: "who, when he was reviled, reviled not again" (when bitterly reproached and insulted, he returned no insult), "when he suffered, he threatened not" (when made to suffer, he uttered no threats of revenge against his persecutors), "but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously"; the witnesses note the secret of this meekness — Christ neither retaliated nor threatened, but "committed himself" (and his cause) into the hands of God, "him that judgeth righteously," trusting the righteous Judge to vindicate him in his own time; so that the believer, wronged and reviled, is to follow Christ in answering not with revenge but with patient trust in the righteous God; committed himself to him that judgeth righteously. The witnesses note who, when he was reviled, reviled not again; when he suffered, he threatened not. The witnesses note but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously.

The witnesses note Christ's perfect meekness — reviled, he reviled not again; suffering, he threatened not, but committed himself to the righteous Judge; answering wrong not with revenge but with trust.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's meekness and trust under wrong. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, reviled and suffering, returned no revenge but committed himself to the righteous Judge, the pattern for the believer. The witnesses note the reviled not again: "when he was reviled, reviled not again" — Gill notes he returned no insult; the reviled not again; the no insult; the no railing; the meek; the reproached. The expositors observe the threatened not: "when he suffered, he threatened not" — no threats of revenge; the threatened not; the no threats; the no revenge; the suffered; the meek. The witnesses press the committed himself: "but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously" — entrusted his cause to God; the committed himself; the his cause; the to God; the entrusted; the trust. The expositors draw the lesson on the righteous Judge: "him that judgeth righteously" — the righteous Judge who will vindicate him; the righteous Judge; the vindicate; the in his time; the God; the trusts. The witnesses observe the no retaliation: he neither retaliated nor threatened; the no retaliation; the not retaliated; the not threatened; the trust; the meek. The expositors note the the pattern: the believer is to follow Christ in patient trust; the the pattern; the follow Christ; the patient trust; the not revenge; the the believer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who when he was reviled reviled not again when he suffered he threatened not, the but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously, and Christ's meekness. The verse thus records Christ's meekness and trust under wrong — teaching that Christ returned no revenge but committed himself to the righteous Judge, the pattern for the believer.
24:
"Who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree, that we, being dead to sins, should live unto righteousness: by whose stripes ye were healed." The witnesses note Christ bearing our sins in his body on the tree. Gill notes this was typified by the high priest bearing the sins of the holy things, and by the scapegoat bearing the iniquities of the people, and foretold by Isaiah; Christ bore the sins of his people in his own body.
