The witnesses note Peter declaring that Christ his own self bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we, dead to sins, should live to righteousness, and by his stripes were healed; the substitutionary sin-bearing of Christ and its saving, sanctifying end — that Peter, rising from Christ's example to his atonement, sets forth the heart of the gospel: "who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree" (Christ in person carried the load of his people's sins, bearing their guilt and its penalty in his own body on the cross, the great act of substitution) — and the twofold end: "that we, being dead to sins, should live unto righteousness" (his death procures our death to sin and life to righteousness, our sanctification) — and the healing: "by whose stripes ye were healed"; the witnesses note the rich truth — Christ's penal, substitutionary sin-bearing both makes atonement for guilt and frees the believer for holy living, and his very wounds ("stripes") are the means of the soul's healing from sin; by whose stripes ye were healed. The witnesses note who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree. The witnesses note that we, being dead to sins, should live unto righteousness: by whose stripes ye were healed.

The witnesses note Christ's substitutionary sin-bearing — bearing our sins in his body on the tree — that we, dead to sin, should live to righteousness; his very wounds the means of the soul's healing.

So the witnesses make the verse the substitutionary sin-bearing of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ bore our sins in his body on the tree, making atonement and freeing us for righteousness, his stripes our healing. The witnesses note the bare our sins: "who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree" — Gill notes Christ carried his people's sins; the bare our sins; the carried; the his people's sins; the the guilt; the the penalty. The expositors observe the in his body: in his own body on the cross, the act of substitution; the in his body; the on the cross; the substitution; the in person; the the tree. The witnesses press the dead to sins: "that we, being dead to sins" — his death procures our death to sin; the dead to sins; the his death; the our death to sin; the procures; the freed. The expositors draw the lesson on the live unto righteousness: "should live unto righteousness" — and life to righteousness, our sanctification; the live unto righteousness; the life; the sanctification; the holy living; the freed. The witnesses observe the stripes: "by whose stripes ye were healed" — his wounds the means of healing; the stripes; the his wounds; the the means; the healing; the the soul. The expositors note the penal substitution: his penal, substitutionary sin-bearing makes atonement; the penal substitution; the atonement; the borne. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that we being dead to sins should live unto righteousness by whose stripes ye were healed, and the sin-bearing of Christ. The verse thus records the substitutionary sin-bearing of Christ — teaching that Christ bore our sins on the tree, making atonement and freeing us for righteousness, his stripes our healing.
25:
"For ye were as sheep going astray; but are now returned unto the Shepherd and Bishop of your souls." The witnesses note the believers' return from straying to the Shepherd of their souls. Gill notes this is a proof of their being healed, namely their conversion; God's elect are sheep before conversion, but as sheep going astray, wandering from God and the fold, till brought back. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they were as sheep going astray, but are now returned to the Shepherd and Bishop of their souls; the return of the straying sheep to Christ their Shepherd and Overseer — that Peter closes the chapter with a tender image of conversion: "for ye were as sheep going astray" (before grace found them, they wandered like lost sheep, straying from God, the fold, and the way, helpless and exposed) "but are now returned unto the Shepherd and Bishop of your souls"; the witnesses note the blessed change and the titles of Christ — they have been brought back (by his grace, for sheep do not find their own way home) to Christ, who is both the "Shepherd" (who feeds, guides, and guards his flock) and the "Bishop" (the Overseer, watching over and caring for their souls); so that the once-straying are now safe under the tender oversight of Christ, the Shepherd and Guardian of the soul; are now returned unto the Shepherd and Bishop of your souls. The witnesses note for ye were as sheep going astray. The witnesses note but are now returned unto the Shepherd and Bishop of your souls.

The witnesses note the return of the straying sheep — once wandering like lost sheep, now brought back to Christ, the Shepherd and Bishop of their souls, who feeds, guides, and watches over them.

So the witnesses make the verse the return to the Shepherd of souls. The lesson the expositors draw is that the once-straying sheep have been brought back to Christ, the Shepherd and Overseer of their souls. The witnesses note the going astray: "for ye were as sheep going astray" — Gill notes they wandered like lost sheep before grace; the going astray; the wandered; the lost sheep; the before grace; the strayed. The expositors observe the helpless: straying from God, the fold, helpless and exposed; the helpless; the from God; the the fold; the the way; the exposed. The witnesses press the returned: "but are now returned" — brought back by grace; the returned; the brought back; the by grace; the not their own way; the home. The expositors draw the lesson on the Shepherd: "unto the Shepherd... of your souls" — Christ who feeds, guides, guards; the Shepherd; the feeds; the guides; the guards; the the flock. The witnesses observe the Bishop: "and Bishop of your souls" — the Overseer watching over their souls; the Bishop; the the Overseer; the watching; the caring; the their souls. The expositors note the now safe: the once-straying are now safe under Christ's oversight; the now safe; the once-straying; the Christ's oversight; the the Guardian; the tender. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for ye were as sheep going astray, the but are now returned unto the Shepherd and Bishop of your souls, and the return to the Shepherd. The verse thus records the return to the Shepherd of souls — teaching that the once-straying sheep have been brought back to Christ, the Shepherd and Overseer of their souls.
